inference_steps: [117s to 119s; 120s to 128s] The relevant scene remains the Inner City Tennis volunteering/drill segment, anchored by the establishing shot naming Inner City Tennis and the indoor-court sequence with the woman in gray in the 120.00s-128.00s window.
[120s to 122s] The woman in gray is the female instructor on the indoor court, wearing a grey t-shirt and black shorts.
[120s to 128s] The new frame retrieval covers the full drill window from 120.00s through 128.00s, so the counting evidence now spans the relevant sequence rather than only an early portion of it.

[123s; 127s; 128s] Within those sampled frames, the woman is visually counted for two ball tosses: one frame appears to show her tossing or about to toss the ball to the child, and another clearly shows her tossing a ball up with her right hand. Other ball-in-air frames are better treated as aftermath or continuation, not separate additional tosses.
[125s] Within the same sampled frames, only one frame is described as a side step or recovery movement, so the grounded side-step count is one.
[123s; 125s; 127s] Using the grounded counts from the sampled full-window frames, the ratio of side steps to ball tosses is 1 to 2.
[123s; 125s; 127s] A 1:2 ratio does not match any of the provided answer choices, so no listed option can be justified from the grounded evidence.

evidence.evidence_text: The queried woman-in-gray Inner City Tennis drill appears in two intervals: 117.00s-119.00s and 120.00s-128.00s.
The 117.00s-119.00s establishing shot shows text identifying the location as Inner City Tennis.
In the indoor court segment, the female instructor wears a grey t-shirt and black shorts.
Retrieved sampled frames at 120.00s, 121.00s, 122.00s, 123.00s, 124.00s, 125.00s, 126.00s, 127.00s, and 128.00s covering the full 120.00s-128.00s drill window.

In the sampled drill frames, one frame is described as clearly showing the woman tossing a ball up with her right hand, and another frame is described as appearing to show her tossing or about to toss the ball to the child. Other ball-related frames are interpretive or may depict the aftermath of a toss rather than a distinct additional toss.
Among the sampled drill frames, only one frame is described as looking like a side step or recovery movement; the evidence does not ground multiple distinct side-step events across the full window.
